text:
HEMATOLOGY / BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT PROGRAM
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
18-MONTH POST-ALLOGENEIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANT

PATIENT: Margaret Chen
DOB: 1977
DIAGNOSIS: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), NOS, de novo; FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio (AR 0.68), DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 S291fs, TET2 Q916*, normal karyotype; ELN 2022 Adverse risk
ATTENDING: [Transplant Hematologist/Oncologist]
VISIT TYPE: Routine survivorship and transplant follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Chen is a 49-year-old female with a history of adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD, high allelic ratio) who presents for her 18-month post-allogeneic stem cell transplant surveillance visit. She has no new complaints. She reports feeling well overall and has returned to her normal activities.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is well known to our transplant program. By way of brief history, she was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with de novo AML at age 48, presenting with symptomatic leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/uL), severe anemia (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelets 32,000/uL), gingival hyperplasia with spontaneous bleeding, and circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy at diagnosis confirmed hypercellular marrow with 72% myeloblasts, Auer rods, and immunophenotype consistent with AML, NOS. NGS demonstrated FLT3-ITD with allelic ratio 0.68 (high), DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 S291fs frameshift, TET2 Q916* (subclonal), and WT1 R462Q -- a heavily mutated, ELN Adverse-risk profile.

She underwent induction chemotherapy with standard 7+3 (cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day x 7 days continuous infusion, daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) plus midostaurin 50 mg BID days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pneumonia (presumed bacterial vs. fungal) managed with cefepime and micafungin, as well as grade 2 mucositis and midostaurin-related nausea. She achieved morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry on day 28 bone marrow biopsy. Given her adverse-risk genomic profile, she proceeded directly to allogeneic SCT without consolidation chemotherapy.

She underwent 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation with myeloablative fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) conditioning (pharmacokinetically dosed), GVHD prophylaxis with tacrolimus and short-course methotrexate, and CMV prophylaxis with letermovir. The donor was a 32-year-old CMV-seropositive male. CD34+ cell dose was 5.2 x 10^6/kg. Post-transplant course was notable for grade 3 mucositis requiring TPN and morphine PCA (resolved by day +18), transient tacrolimus-associated nephrotoxicity (creatinine peaked at 1.4 mg/dL, resolved), and subsequent development of mild chronic GVHD affecting skin (lichenoid papules, biopsy-confirmed, NIH grade 2 on histology) and GI tract (loose stools, NIH score 1). Chimerism at day +30 confirmed 98% donor (myeloid 99%, CD3 92%).

Chronic GVHD was managed with topical clobetasol for skin involvement (excellent response) and oral budesonide 3 mg TID for GI symptoms. She did not require systemic high-dose corticosteroids. Tacrolimus was gradually tapered and successfully discontinued at approximately month 11 without GVHD flare. Budesonide was discontinued at month 9. All prophylactic antimicrobials (letermovir, acyclovir, fluconazole, TMP-SMX) were discontinued per protocol at the 1-year mark. She has remained in morphologic and molecular complete remission with full donor chimerism on all bone marrow assessments through the 12-month biopsy (FLT3-ITD undetectable by qPCR at sensitivity 0.001%; DNMT3A R882H undetectable by ddPCR; RUNX1 frameshift undetectable by NGS deep sequencing; donor chimerism 99.8%).

Today, at 18 months post-transplant, she presents for routine surveillance. Since her last visit (3 months ago), she reports no new constitutional symptoms, no fevers, no night sweats, no unintentional weight loss, and no bleeding. She has resumed full-time teaching at her high school and reports her energy and stamina are at or near her pre-illness baseline. She does note mild ongoing skin dryness on her forearms, which she attributes to residual changes from prior GVHD; there are no new papules or rash. Bowel habits are normal. She denies dysphagia, odynophagia, oral dryness (previously noted, now improved), or ocular symptoms.

She is currently on no immunosuppression. The only regular medications are levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (pre-existing hypothyroidism, dose stable) and low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No unintentional weight loss. Fatigue: mild, improved from prior visits, attributed to normal baseline; no frank fatigue.
HEENT: No oral ulcers. Mild skin dryness noted on forearms (residual). No ocular complaints -- ophthalmology referral is pending for cataract surveillance (busulfan exposure). No xerostomia at this visit (previously mild, now resolved).
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or chest pain. No hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No palpitations, edema, or orthopnea. No chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Normal bowel habits. No diarrhea, constipation, nausea, or abdominal pain. No GI bleeding. No dysphagia.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or urinary frequency.
Musculoskeletal: No joint pain, swelling, or stiffness. No bone pain (important given prior busulfan exposure and concern for avascular necrosis).
Neurologic: No headaches, sensory changes, or cognitive complaints. Returns to normal cognitive function per patient report.
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine. No hot flashes, night sweats, or new hormonal symptoms (menopause screening considered given chemotherapy exposure).
Dermatologic: Residual skin dryness on forearms; no active papules, plaques, or lichen-like lesions. No new skin changes.
Hematologic/Lymphatic: No lymphadenopathy noticed by patient. No bruising or bleeding.
Psychiatric: Mood good. No depression or anxiety reported. Engaged in return to work and normal activities.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: Temperature 36.8°C, Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, Heart rate 72 bpm, Respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 64.2 kg (stable from prior visit), BMI 23.1 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Healthy appearance, appropriate affect. No signs of distress.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Anicteric sclerae. Conjunctivae pink, no pallor. Pupils equal, round, reactive. Oral mucosa moist and intact -- no ulcers, erythema, lichen planus-type changes, or xerostomia. No oral thrush. No gingival hyperplasia (resolved). Oropharynx clear.

Neck: Supple. No cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Thyroid gland not enlarged. No JVD.

Axillae: No axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi. Normal respiratory effort.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly -- spleen not palpable below the left costal margin (previously noted at 4 cm below the LCM at diagnosis, now normalized per prior imaging). Liver edge not palpable. No masses. Active bowel sounds.

Lymph nodes (inguinal): No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No petechiae or purpura. Skin on bilateral forearms with mild post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and residual xerosis; no active papules, active lichenoid lesions, or erosions. No new skin rash.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait steady. No tremor.

MSK: No joint tenderness or swelling. No focal bone tenderness on palpation of vertebrae, sternum, or long bones.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 k/uL (normal)
- ANC: 3,480/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 13.5 g/dL (normal for female; excellent recovery)
- MCV: 88 fL (normal)
- Platelets: 195,000/uL (normal)
- Reticulocytes: 1.2% (normal)
Comment: CBC is entirely within normal limits. Hematopoietic recovery is complete and robust. No cytopenias.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 101 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (stable; no residual tacrolimus nephrotoxicity)
- eGFR: >90 mL/min/1.73m2
- Glucose: 91 mg/dL (fasting, normal)
- Calcium: 9.4 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL (normal)
- AST: 22 U/L (normal)
- ALT: 22 U/L (normal; previously mildly elevated during cGVHD, now normalized)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L (normal)
- Total protein: 7.1 g/dL, Albumin: 4.2 g/dL (normal nutritional status)
- Magnesium: 2.0 mg/dL (normal; no residual tacrolimus-associated hypomagnesemia)

Thyroid Function (annual check):
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (within therapeutic range on levothyroxine)
- Free T4: 1.2 ng/dL (normal)

Peripheral Blood Chimerism (STR analysis):
- Total chimerism: 99.5% donor
- CD3+ T cells: 97% donor
Comment: Full and durable donor chimerism maintained. No mixed chimerism or signal of emerging host hematopoiesis that would raise concern for relapse.

Viral Monitoring:
- CMV PCR (quantitative, peripheral blood): not detected
- EBV PCR (quantitative, peripheral blood): not detected
Comment: No viral reactivation off prophylaxis, now 6 months post-discontinuation of letermovir. CMV immune reconstitution appears adequate.

Fertility/Endocrine (obtained per survivorship protocol in context of myeloablative conditioning and chemotherapy in a 49-year-old woman):
- FSH: 68 mIU/mL (elevated, consistent with primary ovarian insufficiency / treatment-induced menopause)
- Estradiol: <10 pg/mL (menopausal range)
Comment: Consistent with chemotherapy- and conditioning-related ovarian insufficiency, as expected in this age group after myeloablative conditioning with busulfan. Discussed with patient; she had been aware of this possibility. No plans for HRT at this time; referred for gynecology consultation as part of survivorship care.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging obtained at today's visit. The most recent imaging was a whole-body PET-CT performed at the 1-year post-transplant timepoint, which showed complete metabolic response with no FDG-avid lesions, normal spleen and liver size, and complete resolution of previously noted minor pulmonary ground-glass opacities. That scan was entirely reassuring. No imaging is indicated at this routine 18-month visit in the absence of clinical signs or symptoms of relapse.

Bone mineral density (DEXA scan): ordered at the prior visit, results reviewed today. L-spine T-score: -1.3 (osteopenia range). Left femoral neck T-score: -1.1 (osteopenia range). Comment: low bone density consistent with busulfan-based myeloablative conditioning, corticosteroid exposure (topical only, but prior GVHD treatment), and treatment-associated ovarian insufficiency. No frank osteoporosis at this time. Vitamin D and calcium supplementation recommended; bisphosphonate therapy deferred for now, to be reassessed after gynecology evaluation and potential HRT discussion.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. AML, adverse-risk (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 S291fs, TET2 Q916*), status post 7+3 + midostaurin induction and matched unrelated donor allogeneic SCT (FluBu4 conditioning, day 0 approximately 18 months ago): Ms. Chen remains in complete remission. Her most recent bone marrow assessment at 12 months post-transplant was morphologically and molecularly negative (FLT3-ITD undetectable, DNMT3A undetectable, RUNX1 undetectable by highly sensitive assays), and peripheral blood chimerism today is 99.5% donor. She has no clinical, laboratory, or imaging features to suggest relapse. CBC is entirely normal. She has crossed the 18-month mark in complete remission after a de novo adverse-risk AML diagnosis -- a gratifying milestone given the historically poor prognosis associated with her mutational profile.
   - No bone marrow biopsy is indicated today. Per current protocol, biopsy at 18 months post-transplant is obtained only if clinically indicated (no indication currently -- normal CBC, full chimerism, no symptoms).
   - Continue surveillance with clinic visit and CBC/CMP every 3 months.
   - Peripheral blood chimerism to be repeated at next visit (21 months).
   - If CBC remains stable and no signs of relapse, formal 2-year post-transplant evaluation with bone marrow biopsy and MRD assessment is planned.

2. Chronic GVHD, mild, skin and GI (resolved): cGVHD has been in complete remission. Skin: no active lesions, only residual post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and mild xerosis. No topical corticosteroids currently in use (discontinued). GI: normal bowel function since budesonide was stopped at month 9. No immunosuppression ongoing.
   - Continue clinical monitoring at each visit.
   - No GVHD-directed therapy indicated at this time.
   - Patient counseled to report any recurrence of skin rash, worsening GI symptoms, oral mucosal changes, ocular symptoms, or dyspnea (late pulmonary GVHD), as these could represent cGVHD flare off immunosuppression.

3. Post-transplant survivorship:
   a. Infections: Off all prophylaxis. CMV and EBV undetectable today. Immune reconstitution appears good based on clinical trajectory and absence of infections over the past 6 months. Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, zoster) are to be deferred until at least 24 months post-transplant (and confirmed off immunosuppression with adequate immune reconstitution). Killed/inactivated vaccines are appropriate starting at 12 months; updated flu vaccine recommended this season. Pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccines should be re-administered per post-transplant vaccination schedule (reviewed with patient and schedule provided).
   b. Bone health: Osteopenia on DEXA (T-scores -1.3 and -1.1). Initiated cholecalciferol 2000 IU daily and calcium carbonate 1250 mg daily. Referred to gynecology for evaluation of treatment-related ovarian insufficiency and discussion of hormone replacement therapy, which may also benefit bone health. Bisphosphonate therapy to be reconsidered in 6-12 months based on repeat DEXA and gynecology recommendations.
   c. Ophthalmology: Referral placed for cataract surveillance (busulfan conditioning increases cataract risk). Patient has not yet been seen; asked to follow up on scheduling.
   d. Thyroid function: TSH 2.1, stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Continue current dose.
   e. Reproductive/gynecologic: FSH 68 and estradiol <10, consistent with treatment-induced ovarian insufficiency/premature menopause. Referral placed to gynecologic oncology or gynecology for survivorship counseling. Discussed risk of osteoporosis, cardiovascular health, sexual health, and role of potential HRT in a post-transplant context.
   f. Cardiovascular: No concerns. BP 118/74. No metabolic syndrome features. Aspirin 81 mg daily continued (general cardiovascular prophylaxis). Diet and exercise counseling provided. Moderate aerobic activity encouraged.
   g. Secondary malignancy surveillance: Counseled regarding increased risk of secondary malignancies post-allogeneic SCT (EBV-associated lymphoproliferative disease, myelodysplasia from donor cells, solid tumors from prior chemotherapy and radiation). Annual skin examination is recommended (particularly given prior GVHD, which is a risk factor for squamous cell carcinoma). Annual gynecologic care. Age-appropriate colorectal cancer screening to be initiated at 50 (in approximately 1 year) per standard guidelines (no additional risk-stratification needed at this time beyond standard post-transplant protocols). Cervical cancer screening up to date per patient report.
   h. Quality of life / return to function: The patient has returned to full-time teaching, which is an excellent marker of functional recovery. Mood appears good. No active symptoms of depression or anxiety. She declines formal psychosocial intervention at this time but is aware of available survivorship resources including survivorship clinic and social work.

4. Hypothyroidism (pre-existing): Stable. TSH 2.1 on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Continue current dose. Annual TSH monitoring.

Medications at end of visit:
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily
- Cholecalciferol 2000 IU PO daily (NEW - initiated today for osteopenia)
- Calcium carbonate 1250 mg PO daily with meals (NEW - initiated today for osteopenia)

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Follow-up:
- Next hematology/transplant visit in 3 months (21-month post-transplant)
- CBC with differential, CMP, peripheral blood chimerism at next visit
- Formal 2-year evaluation with bone marrow biopsy and comprehensive molecular MRD panel planned
- Ophthalmology appointment pending
- Gynecology referral placed today
- Patient instructed to call or message with any new fevers, bruising, bleeding, fatigue, skin rashes, or other concerning symptoms before next scheduled visit

Time spent: 45 minutes (>50% time spent on counseling and coordination of care).

[Electronically signed by Attending Transplant Hematologist/Oncologist]
